Caution: when you point your agent to someone else's code, you have to comply with the other code base's license, including proper attribution. Caution: if the other's code is bad and have design issues, an agent can fix surface-level bugs, but for re-architecturing the code, you will have to properly guide it. Problem: now it's too easy to reuse, recycle, repackage open-source code, even for our competitors. IDENTIAL & PROPRIETARY

When you use sanitizers in C++, all code must be compiled with sanitizers, including the code in Rust. For example, MSan requires every memory write to be instrumented, and ASan requires instrumenting every memory allocation/deallocation. This is another reason for compiling everything from source. Sanitizers in Rust were a problem in 2022, but in 2025 it is as easy as specifying the right toolchain for Rust. Ill: Click House

aae8013e8b7b281fe485a128ae4e4dee75ff508a.text: Prototyping new features Cache for deserialized columns #82335 15 #96844 ps alexey-milovidov opened on Jun 21, 2025 - edited by alexey-milovidov Edits » Member Company or project name ClickHouse Use case Goals: + Make MergeTree performance no less than that of Memory tables. + Provide the first step towards the cache for intermediate data in the query pipeline. Reading data from a MergeTree table requires: + reading compressed data from the virtual filesystem or page cache (unless we use uncompressed cache); «by the way, reading from the Linux page cache does a memory copy from the kernel to userspace (unless we use userspace page cache); ‘+ decompressing this data (unless we use uncompressed cache); ‘+ deserializing it into columns, which involves a memory copy or a slightly more complex operation (for ColumnString) (unless the block sizes align nicely so that the readsig method is used, but itis rare); With the columns cache, we can eliminate all these steps for hot data. Describe the solution you'd like The cache has the following structure: Table UID > data part > column name -> mark range > ColumnPtr ‘Add settings use_colunns_cache , enable_writes_to_colums_cache , enable_reads_from_columns_cache , colunn_cache_max_size_in_bytes , colunn_cache_nax_entries , similarly to query cache. When we read certain columns in certain mark ranges in data parts, if enable _reads_from columns cache is set, we first locate intersecting mark ranges in the cache. If they were found, we take them from the cache, and if necessary, cut to adjust to the requested mark range. Ranges not presented in the cache will be read as usual. The max_block_size setting is ignored in favor of returning the cached data as is. Typical mistake: — the author tested the hash function on every input size; — the inputs of 1 byte, 2 bytes, ... 111 bytes, etc; ... but did not test in a loop with a random distribution of input sizes. — using the same input size in a loop makes CPU branch predictor works too good; ... but did not test in a loop with a not so random distribution of input sizes. — what if a real dataset has 90% strings of size zero, 9% strings of size 1..50 and 1% of strings around 1KB? ; Ill: ClickHouse

It requires a gargantuan Cl system, that runs everything under ASan, TSan, MSan, UBSan, Every day Cl runs about 10..30 million tests in 145 commits and 70 pull requests on average: e Functional tests (End-to-End tests for ClickHouse server and Client) Integration tests (End-to-End tests for ClickHouse in a cluster, Kafka, MySQL, Postgres, MongoDB, Redis, HDFS, Azure, S3, RabbitMQ, LDAP, Kerberos, etc) Unit tests (for interfaces in the code) Performance tests (for performance regressions and benchmarks) Stress tests (correctness under high randomized load) Fuzzing (correctness under randomly modified queries) Compatibility and Installation tests. Builds for different platforms. Logic tests (logical correctness of the query engine: SQLancer, SQLTest) Jepsen tests (correctness of the distributed coordination) Style tests. Ill: Click House

e73ad717230ecbf8871c2ba2632a547370a8d456.text: Always Dig Into Details Recipes: Always-on profiling. Telemetry and introspection. — What every thread on the server is doing now? SELECT arrayStringConcat(arrayMap( x > demangle(addressToSymbol(x)), trace), '\n') FROM system.stack_trace — Where the query spend most of the time? SELECT arrayStringConcat(symbols, '\n') FROM system.trace_log WHERE query_id = '..' — How many resources the query consumed? SELECT ProfileEvents FROM system.query_log WHERE query_id = '...' effd1899b80ea84612fb5446d72ee9ff9258c3b8.text: Design For Production Workloads There are many database projects designed to work in specific environments: - FPGA databases; GPU databases; - databases for zoned SSDs; programmable SSDs; - databases requiring RDMA over Infiniband; - databases relying on 100% reliable network; - hardware transactional memory and persistent memory; - high socket-count systems, mainframes... They don't survive. A good database must scale on common hardware. ClickHouse works on x86_64, AArch64, RISC-V; Linux, Mac, and FreeBSD. Ill: ClickHouse

f0abf02f511c0b5396df24083ca8aa279b1f4fe4.text: Be Ready For New Frontiers ClickHouse is a good database for real time analytics. But now, it is also the best database for agentic analytics! ClickHouse is a good database for observability. But now, it is also the best database for analytics on semistructured data! But now, it is also the best database for Al SRE. The database landscape is always changing, and there always be something new - we always have to adapt. Ill: ClickHouse

f0f0588b5ad867a6eb14c28c087ac9db09be1233.text: How to build a great database? — It takes time: “The Vertica Analytic Database: C-Store 7 Years Later", PVLDB Vol. 5, No. 12 (2012) — It takes effort: “We built an initial prototype, putting in the first 90% of the effort required to create a real system, and it more or less worked. | think that the thing that distinguished INGRES from the typical academic project, and in retrospect one of the smartest things we ever did, was to then put in the next 90% of the effort to make INGRES really work.” - Michael Stonebraker — It takes persistency: There is a huge number of good databases, that their authors stopped developing, and they don’t thrive anymore: Greenplum, Vertica, InfiniDB, SingleStore, kdb ... Ill: ClickHouse
